tate of Califorrtia 
Fair Political Practices CommissioIl. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100"K STREEi BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
Administration 
Executive/legal 
321-5901 
Enforcement 
Statements of Economic Interest 
916) 322·5662 
Douglas Dang 
Peter W. Olson 
322·5660 
City Attorney's Office 
330 West Twentieth Avenue 
San Mateo, CA 
94403 
322·6441 
322·6444 
October 13, 1983 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-204 
Dear Messrs. Dang and Olson: 
Thank you for your request for advice on behalf of the 
Chairman of the San Mateo City Planning Commission, Mr. John 
Raiser. 
The facts, as stated in your letter, are that the City 
is presently in the process of preparing a downtown specific 
plan. 
This plan, which is authorized under Government Code 
Section 65450, et ~.,l/ will be very detailed in nature and 
is intended to allow the City of San Mateo to execute its 
general plan in the downtown area. 
The stated goal of the 
development plan will be to maintain the "downtown as a viable 
and competitive retail center" and to maintain the "sources of 
demand for office space." 
The plan draft now states that the: 
Downtown should continue to grow and develop to be 
an important business, financial, medical services and 
retailing center of the City and of the mid-county. 
Priority should be given to new office and retail 
development in locations and at densities supportive of 
the increased attraction of downtown as a business and 
retail center. 
Development should be channeled to 
permit further expansion beyond that period. 
Currently, citizens' groups, consultants and the Planning 
Commission's staff are preparing the plan. 
Upon its completion, 
it will be submitted to the Planning Commission, which will hold 
public hearings, amend the plan as it sees fit, and send it to 
1/ All references are to the Government Code unless 
otherwise stated. 


Douglas Dang 
Peter W. Olson 
October 13, 1983 
Page 2 
the City Council for further review, amendment and action. 
The 
area that will be covered by the plan is approximately 53 city 
blocks and includes about 900 parcels of land. 
It is anticipated that the draft which will be presented to 
the Planning Commission will, among other things, identify 
downtown areas to be given certain height or density 
limitations; specify areas for certain types of development, 
such as retail, residential, or banks; specify certain areas for 
beautification projects; indicate changes in traffic patterns; 
and indicate areas for zoning changes. 
Planning Commissioner Raiser has asked whether the Political 
Reform Act requires him to disqualify himself from the Planning 
Commission's consideration of the proposed plan due to the 
financial interests that he has in and around the planning 
area. 
Mr. Raiser's interests are as follows: 
Within the Downtown Planning Area: 
1. 
Mr. Raiser owns 100 percent of Raiser Construction Co., 
Inc., which owns the following parcels of land within the 
downtown plan area: 
a. 
800 Claremont Street South (100% equity interest) 
-- a modern 2-story office building known as San Mateo 
Square. 
This building is the headquarters for Raiser 
Construction Co., Inc. 
The building's remaining office 
space is leased. 
b. 
700 Claremont Street South (100% equity interest) 
-- a 2-story building which is part of the San Mateo Square 
office development. 
It is leased for office space. 
2. 
Mr. Raiser is president and 100 percent owner of Park 
Laurel Corporation which owns the following parcels of land 
within the downtown plan area: 
601, 611 and 619 Laurel and 214 - 6th Avenue. 
This 
string of lots, which are now single-family homes, were 
going to be developed as condominiums, but the application 
lapsed. 
Near the Downtown Planning Area: 
The following properties are within one or two blocks of the 
downtown planning area: 


Douglas Dang 
Peter W. Olson 
October 13, 1983 
Page 3 
a. 
104 Hayward Avenue -- a residential home owned by 
Raiser Construction Co., Inc. 
b. 
210 Mt. Diablo -- a 17-unit apartment building 100% 
owned by Raiser Construction Co., Inc. 
c. 
32 San Mateo Drive -- through a partnership, 
Mr. Raiser owns a 50% interest in this professional office 
building. 
DISCUSSION 
Section 87100 provides that no public official shall make or 
participate in the making of a governmental decision if he knows 
or has reason to know that he has a financial interest in the 
decision. 
An official has a "financial interest" within the 
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,ll on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth more 
than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income . • . aggregating two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided 
to ... the public official within 12 months prior to 
the time when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
(Section 87103) 
In the present case, although the Planning Commission 
members will not make the final decisions concerning the content 
of the plan, they will "participate" in these decisions by 
reviewing the contents, amending them, and submitting the plan 
to the City Council. 
(See the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section l8700(c) (2) (B).) 
Once Mr. Raiser receives the completed 
II See the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703 which discusses the "public generally" exception. 


Douglas Dang 
Peter W. Olson 
October 13, 1983 
Page 4 
draft of the plan, he must determine which decisions he may 
participate in and which he may not. 
This letter is intended to 
provide him with guidance in making this determination. 
Real Property Interests: 
Mr. Raiser has real property interests worth over $1,000 in 
all of the pieces of property owned by Raiser Construction Co., 
Inc., Park Laurel Corporation and the partnership because the 
Political Reform Act provides that interests in real property of 
an individual include a pro rata share of any real property 
interests owned by any business entity in which the individual 
owns a 10 percent interest or greater. 
(Section 82033.) 
Mr. Raiser must disqualify himself from a decision 
concerning the specific downtown plan if (1) it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect on one of his real property interests, and (2) the effect 
of the decision on his property will be distinguishable from its 
effect on the public generally. 
These determinations must be 
based upon the specific facts involved in the decision. 
A decision will have a material financial effect on a piece 
of real property if it will increase or decrease: 
(A) 
The income producing potential of the 
property by the lesser of: 
1. 
One thousand dollars ($1,000) per month; 
or 
2. 
Five percent per month if the effect is 
fifty dollars ($50) or more per month; or 
(B) 
The fair market value of the property by the 
lesser of: 
1. 
Ten thousand dollars ($10,000); or 
2. 
One half of one percent if the effect is 
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (2) (A), (B).) 
In deciding whether it is foreseeable that a decision will have 
a material financial effect, it may be helpful to consult your 
City's assessor. 


Douglas Dang 
Pete r W. Olson 
October 13, 1983 
Page 5 
Such a decision will not require Mr. Raiser's disquali-
fication, however, if it will affect all of the property in the 
downtown area in substantially the same manner. 
For example, a 
decision on whether to impose a water surcharge of $5 per month 
on each water user in the downtown area would not require his 
disqualification. 
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18703.) 
However, if the effect on Mr. Raiser's property will be 
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally - that 
is, from its effect on the entire public or a significant 
segment of the public - disqualification is required. 
For 
example, Mr. Raisec may not participate in a decision which will 
foreseeably have a material financial effect only on 
Mr. Raiser's property, or on Mr. Raisers property and a few 
other pieces of property. 
In the case of decisions which have a similar effect on a 
substantial number, but not all, of the property in the downtown 
area, Mr. Raiser should consult us for specific advice. 
Investment Interests and Business Positions: 
Mr. Raiser has an investment interest worth over $1,000 in 
Raiser Construction Co., Inc., Park Laurel Corporation and the 
partnership, and he holds business positions in each of them. 
Therefore, in addition to disqualification from decisions 
affecting his real property interests, he must disqualify 
himself from considering any aspect of the proposed downtown 
plan that will foreseeably have a material financial effect on 
these companies distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally. 
The decision's effect on an investment interest will be 
material if it will increase or decrease: 
of: 
(A) 
The annualized gross revenues by the lesser 
1. 
One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) i 
or 
2. 
One percent if the effect is one thousand 
dollars ($1,000) or morei or 
(B) 
Annual net income by the lesser of: 
1. 
Fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) i or 


Douglas Dang 
Peter W. Olson 
October 13, 1983 
Page 6 
of: 
2. 
One half of one percent if the effect is 
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more; or 
(C) 
Current assets or liabilities by the lesser 
1. 
One hundred thousand dollars ($100,000) 
or 
2. 
One half of one percent if the effect is 
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
Current assets are deemed to be decreased by 
the amount of any expenses incurred as a result of 
a governmental decision. 
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (l)(A), (B), (C).) 
In determining whether a decision's effect on an investment 
interest is distinguishable from the effect on the public 
generally or on a significant segment of the public generally, 
the analysis used in the preceding section of this letter should 
be applied. 
Source of Income: 
If any of the business entities in which Mr. Raiser has an 
investment interest are also sources of income to him of $250 or 
more, he has an additional interest in these companies that may 
necessitate his disqualification from the consideration of 
certain aspects of the plan. 
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702(b) (3).) 
If any of the tenants in the buildings owned by Raiser 
Construction Co., Inc., Park Laurel Corporation, or the partner-
ship, paid sufficient rent to the company owning the building so 
that Mr. Raiser's pro rata share of the rent amounts to $250 or 
more, the tenant is a source of income to Mr. Raiser and he 
cannot consider any recommendation in the plan if it will 
foreseeably have a material financial effect on the tenant that 
is different from the effect on the public generally. 
You indicated in a telephone conversation that the Planning 
Commission is procedurally flexible in its consideration of the 
downtown specific plan. 
Because of Mr. Raiser's extensive 
interests in the downtown area, it seems likely that there will 
be decisions which will require his disqualification. 
In order 
to allow him to participate in those aspects of the plan which 


Doug las Dang 
Peter W. Olson 
October 13, 1983 
Page 7 
do not require his disqualification, we recommend that the other 
Commissioners first resolve the matters in which Mr. Raiser has 
a conflict. 
He will then be able to participate in the 
remaining matters and in any overall vote11 to approve the 
recommendations that will be sent to the City Council.if 
I hope that this letter will be of help to you. 
If you have 
any additional questions, please feel free to contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosure 
Very truly yours, 
11 
He cannot participate in the overall vote unless the 
matters in which he has a conflict are no longer subject to 
change. 
il For other methods for isolating conflicts of 
interests, see the enclosed copy of the advice letter to Kenneth 
MOY· 


OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY 
Executive Director 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 
95804 
330 WEST TWENTIETH AVENUE 
SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA 94403 
TELEPHONE: (415) 574·6720 
August 31, 1983 
Re: 
Request for Advice on Potential Conflict of Interest 
Dear Sir: 
This letter is a request for advice by the City Attorney 
of the City of San Mateo, on behalf of John Raiser, planning 
commissioner for the City of San Mateo. 
The City of San Mateo is presently in the process of 
drafting a downtown specific plan. 
There are approximately 900 parcels of land within this 
downtown planning area contained in about 53 city blocks. 
The 
lowest zoning classification at present in the downtown planning 
area is R-3 (residential areas above duplex level), with most 
of the property zoned well above that. 
The work order for the downtown specific plan to the 
City's consultants at paragraph 2.3 provides that the plan is to 
maintain "downtown as a viable and competitive retail center 
and sources of demand for office space." 
The plan draft now 
states at page 4 that: 
"Downtown should continue to grow and develop to 
be an important business, financial, medical services 
and retailing center of the City and of the mid-county. 
Priority should be given to new office and retail 
development in locations and at densities supportive 
of the increased attraction of downtown as a business 
and retail center. 
Development within the next 20 
years should be channeled to permit further expansion 
beyond that period." 


Executive Director 
Fair Political Practic 
Cqmm~ 
August 31, 1983 
Subsequent implementation of this downtown specific plan 
by legislation may result in increased densities, changed traffic 
patterns, higher height limits, beautification projects, and even 
new zoning classifications. 
The City Planning COlnmission will not make the final decision 
on the downtown specific plan. 
It will review the recommendations 
sent to it by the consultant through staff and various other 
citizen commissions now reviewing the plan. 
The Commission will 
then have public hearings and amend the plan as it sees fit. 
The 
plan will then be sent forward on the recommendation of the 
Commission to the City Council for further review, amendment, and 
action. 
Planning Commissioner Raiser now owns or controls 100% 
of Raiser Construction Co. Inc., which owns the following parcels 
of land within the downtown plan area: 
SOO Claremont Street South (100% equity interest 
listed) -- presently a modern 2-story office building 
known as San Mateo Square. 
This building headquarters 
Raiser Construction Co., and has leased office space 
in the building. 
700 Claremont Street South (100% equity interest 
listed) -- 2-story building which is part of the San 
Mateo Square office development, it is largely leased 
out for office space. 
Within 2 blocks of the downtown plan area, the company 
also owns: 
104 Hayward Avenue -- a residential home. 
In addition, John Raiser is president and sole shareholder 
of Park Laurel Corporation which owns 601, 611, and 619 Laurel 
and 214 - 6th Avenue, within the downtown specific plan area. 
Pursuant to a 19S1 planning application, this string of properties 
was to be converted to 655 Laurel as a condominium development, 
but that application has now lapsed. 
Commissioner Raiser has 
excess of $100,000 valued interest in Park Laurel Corporation. 
Presently the properties are single-family homes. 
Other properties near the downtown plan area are: 
1. 
32 No. San Mateo Drive 
a. 
50% interest. 
Owns individually a share in a 
partnership with his equity valued in excess of $10,000 
but less than $100,000 in a professional office building. 


Executive Director 
Fair Political Practices Comm. 
-3-
August 31, 1983 
2. 
210 Mt. Diablo 
a. 
17 unit apartment. 
Raiser Construction owns 
100% equity valued in excess of $100,000. 
These locations are placed in the enclosed map of the 
downtown specific plan area. 
The City Attorney's office is aware of the Commission's 
opinions in In re Gillmor (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 38, and In re Brown 
(1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 19. 
There is no quorum requirement for Commissioner Raiser to 
participate in the Planning Commission process. 
Because of the vital, long-range importance of the downtown 
specific plan, this office and Commissioner Raiser want to ensure 
that the plan finally established is valid in every particular. 
Therefore, this office requests advice on the following two questions: 
1. 
Must Commissioner Raiser disqualify himself as a 
commissioner from the consideration and recommendation Drocess of 
the San Mateo Planning Commission on the downtown specific plan for 
San Mateo? 
2. 
If Commissioner Raiser disqualifies himself from sitting 
as a commissioner on the downtown specific plan, may he appear as 
a member of the general public before the Planning Commission and/or 
City Council to represent his own private interests? 
Thank you for your consideration of these questions. 
If there 
is any further information you may require, please do not hesitate 
to contact me. 
By

Enclosures 
DOUGLAS DANG 
CITY ATTORNEY 
CITY OF SAN MATEO 
ATTORNEY 






P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Administration 
(916) 322·5662 
322.5660 
Peter W. Olson 
Assistant City Attorney 
330 West Twentieth Avenue 
San Mateo, CA 
94403 
Dear Mr. Olson: 
Executive/legal 
32~·5901 
Enfarcement 
322-6441 
October 6, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322·6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-204 
This letter is to confirm our conversation in which you 
granted me an extension of time to reply to Mr. Raiser's 
questions regarding the Political Reform Act. 
This extension 
was necessary due to the complexity Qf the issues and my need 
for additional information. 
As I assured you on the telephone, 
Mr. Raiser will be provided with guidance prior to the time of 
his decision making. 
JSM:plh 
Very truly yours, 
:1tuU6 
Jani s Shank 
Counsel 
Legal Division

sections.conclusion: Mr. Raiser must disqualify himself from a decision concerning the specific downtown plan if (1) it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one of his real property interests, and (2) the effect of the decision on his property will be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. These determinations must be based upon the specific facts involved in the decision. Due to Mr. Raiser's extensive interests in the downtown area, it seems likely that there will be decisions which will require his disqualification. The Commission should first resolve matters in which Mr. Raiser has a conflict, allowing him to participate in remaining matters and any overall vote.

sections.question_synthetic: Must a Planning Commission member disqualify himself from voting on a downtown specific plan under the Political Reform Act due to his substantial real property and business interests within the planning area?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Raiser must disqualify himself from specific decisions if they will foreseeably have a material financial effect on his real property or business interests that is distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. The Planning Commission should sequentially address conflicted matters first, then allow Mr. Raiser to participate in remaining decisions and final approval votes.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: ocr, old; LLM: Document is OCR'd with minor formatting irregularities typical of 1983 era correspondence. The question and conclusion sections are clearly identifiable. The document provides detailed guidance on material financial effect thresholds and the public generally exception. There is a minor truncation at the very end of the provided text that does not affect extraction of main content.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s regarding the Political Reform Act. 
This extension 
was necessary due to the complexity Qf the issues and my need 
for additional information. 
As I assured you on the telephone, 
Mr. Raiser will be provided with guidance prior to the time of 
his decision making. 
JSM:plh

CONCLUSION: Mr. Raiser must disqualify himself from a decision concerning the specific downtown plan if (1) it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one of his real property interests, and (2) the effect of the decision on his property will be distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. These determinations must be based upon the specific facts involved in the decision. Due to Mr. Raiser's extensive interests in the downtown area, it seems likely that there will be decisions which will require his disqualification. The Commission should first resolve matters in which Mr. Raiser has a conflict, allowing him to participate in remaining matters and any overall vote.

The area that will be covered by the plan is approximately 53 city blocks and includes about 900 parcels of land. It is anticipated that the draft which will be presented to the Planning Commission will, among other things, identify downtown areas to be given certain height or density limitations; specify areas for certain types of development, such as retail, residential, or banks; specify certain areas for beautification projects; indicate changes in traffic patterns; and indicate areas for zoning changes. Planning Commissioner Raiser has asked whether the Political Reform Act requires him to disqualify himself from the Planning Commission's consideration of the proposed plan due to the financial interests that he has in and around the planning area. Mr. Raiser's interests are as follows: Within the Downtown Planning Area: 1. Mr. Raiser owns 100 percent of Raiser Construction Co., Inc., which owns the following parcels of land within the downtown plan area: a. 800 Claremont Street South (100% equity interest) -- a modern 2-story office building known as San Mateo Square. This building is the headquarters for Raiser Construction Co., Inc. The building's remaining office
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter regarding disqualification requirements for Planning Commissioner Raiser in connection with a downtown specific plan for San Mateo. The letter analyzes his real property interests, investment interests, business positions, and potential income sources under Political Reform Act Section 87100 and applicable administrative code sections.
